metadata.title: A Problem Well Stated
core_topics: Design problem analysis and brief decomposition
UAE cultural heritage and context in game design
Identifying problem, audience, purpose, and constraints
How Large Language Models predict the next token
AI settings: Temperature, Top-K, and Top-P
User experience and empathy in game design
ai_focus: How Large Language Models predict the next token using patterns learned from training data
AI temperature settings (low vs. high) and their effect on output predictability and creativity
Top-K filtering — limiting token selection to the top k most likely tokens
Top-P (nucleus) sampling — probability-based filtering of token selection
Why generative AI produces plausible but contextually incorrect outputs (probabilistic, not factual)
Critical comparison of AI-generated summaries against human analysis
Prompt engineering for heritage project summaries

OECD AI Literacy framework references: K1.2 (AI generates outputs without intent or awareness), K1.3 (generative AI produces probabilistic outputs without understanding), K4.1 (AI lacks context, judgment, and originality)
AI bias checking and stereotype detection in persona creation
learning_objectives: Understand what a design problem is and identify its key elements (problem, audience, purpose, constraints) in UAE-based briefs.
Recognise how real-world UAE challenges connect to meaningful game-design opportunities, and select one brief that motivates you.
metadata.activity_type: mixed — group brief immersion with individual portfolio documentation
metadata.activity_description: The lesson begins with a Starter 'Fall in Love with the Problem' (3 minutes), where students discuss quotes from Charles Kettering and Albert Einstein about problem-focused design, then identify the core problem solved by their favourite game. Activity 1.1 – Problem in My Favourite Game (Assessment for Learning): Students describe the problem their favourite game solves and distinguish between problems and features. Portfolio Entry 1 – Brief Analysis (Assessment of Learning): Students receive multiple UAE heritage-themed design briefs, act as 'brief detectives' to identify five key elements (Problem, Audience, Purpose, Constraints, Success Criteria), and complete a structured analysis table. The class uses a Jigsaw method where groups cover separate briefs and then regroup to share findings. Activity 1.2 – Keyword & Pattern Extraction (Assessment for Learning): Students identify recurring keywords across all briefs and group them into patterns such as cultural identity, sustainability, and innovation, followed by a class discussion on shared UAE design values. Activity 1.3 – Five Key Questions Framework (Assessment for Learning): Students interrogate their selected brief across five dimensions: Why (impact), Who (users and stakeholders), What tools/constraints, When/Success, and What if/Consequences/Pivot. An optional AI Extension Prompt asks students to summarise a heritage project in 100 words and compare the AI output with their own analysis. Reflection / Plenary – My Design Purpose: Students identify the problem they are most motivated to solve, complete AI literacy reflection sentence starters ('Today AI helped me by…' and 'I still needed to decide…'), and self-assess using All/Most/Some criteria. Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson (10 minutes, optional): Students explore how Large Language Models predict the next token, experiment with a Transformer demo adjusting Temperature, Top-K, and Top-P settings, then answer four reflection questions comparing AI prediction to their own pattern-finding work. Endstar Warm-Up (if time allows): Students choose between a Logic Loop Challenge (rebuild a trigger-to-door-open sequence) or an Environment Sprint (create a mini scene with up to 6 props). The lesson closes with three AI Literacy MCQ exit-ticket questions completed in Classkick.

Activity 1.2 – Keyword & Pattern Extraction notes
Activity 1.3 – Five Questions Framework table
My Design Purpose reflection

Self-Assessment (All/Most/Some)

AI Literacy MCQ exit-ticket questions (3 per lesson) completed in Classkick
Self-assessment using All/Most/Some criteria at lesson close

teacher_notes.notes: Teaching note: Before starting any work please have the students complete the diagnostic here. They will repeat the same set of questions in lesson 24.

Ask students to briefly brainstorm some real-world problems they encounter and how these might be turned into game challenges. Encourage them to think about player motivation. Context: Discuss how this concept applies within the UAE's specific cultural or social contexts. (Refer to relevant UAE context link, if applicable). In game design, the most compelling experiences often stem from exploring real-world problems and designing for real players, creating challenges that resonate and deeply engage.

Display this slide immediately after the Learning Objectives. Revisit it mid-lesson to check which criteria students feel confident about. Inspectors should see learners able to recall one or more criteria in their own words. This links directly to the Product Development Cycle and IB Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing.

Teacher Note: Facilitate a 3-minute class discussion before the mini-task. Collect 3-4 examples to share aloud. Emphasise that problems don't have to be serious — entertainment and fun are valid problems to solve.

Teacher should split the class into groups so that each group covers a separate brief. Jigsaw method of teaching can be used here to research and then regroup as an entire class to share their research and insights. Teacher Note: Provide printed/digital versions of the briefs with wide margins for annotation. Model colour-coding/numbering with one brief on the board first. Circulate to check students are identifying elements correctly.

The teacher should allocate a brief to each group so that all briefs get analyzed during this lesson. NOTE** The students can analyze more than their allocated brief outside of the teaching time. Each student must have analyzed at least one brief in this lesson.
Infographic for display purposes only, the teacher can introduce the briefs and link them UAE places of interest.

Teacher can led the activity here by explaining pattern recognition and detection as a wider concept in the field of CS. Teacher can then assign the activity to the students to see if they can identify patterns, sequences and general insights from the briefs provided. Students can then answer in the provided section Activity. Teacher Note: Provide printed/digital briefs with wide margins for annotation. Model colour-coding with one brief on the board first. Circulate to check students are identifying elements correctly.
Teacher Note: Model completing one row of the framework as a class using a sample brief. Students then complete their own table independently. Peer-check in pairs before moving to reflection.

Teacher Note: Create a safe space for sharing. Emphasise that motivation can be simple ('I think it sounds fun') or profound ('My grandmother lost her memory and I want to preserve stories'). Collect portfolio entries to inform brief selection.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today's criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

Teacher Note: Facilitate the discussion on why AI can be wrong. Why Generative AI Can Be Wrong: Because AI predicts what sounds correct, not what is correct; fills gaps using probability, not real understanding; can misread context or skip important details; may produce confident answers that are inaccurate. This is why human skills—pattern-spotting, insight-finding, and structuring information—are essential. They help you decide when AI is trustworthy and when it isn't.
Teacher Note: Explain the system settings temperature, TOP-K and Top-P. Allow the students to get active and change these using their own devices and user prompts.

Teacher Note: Sample responses. 1. Low temperature (around 0.2) made the AI most predictable because the model chooses the safest, most likely next token and avoids creative or unusual options. 2. High temperature (around 1.0) made the AI more creative or random because the model is allowed to take riskier choices and pick words that are less common or less expected. 3. They may want predictable behaviour for things like tutorials, NPC dialogue, safety, or consistency. Too much randomness can break the experience or confuse players. 4. Just like we looked for patterns and structured information in the design brief, the AI also looks for patterns in the text to decide what comes next. Both processes use clues from earlier information to make the best prediction.

Endless studios session, if you have three lessons per week this extra lesson can be dedicated studio lessons where students design mock up and build in Endstar. Teacher Note: Use only if time allows or to support kinaesthetic learners.

metadata.title: Who Are We Designing For?
core_topics: Product Development Cycle – Empathise / Define phase
Cultural sensitivity and representation in AI
ai_focus: AI-generated personas and bias checking — students use AI to draft a persona then critique it for stereotypes and missing cultural perspectives
AI bias from unrepresentative training data — how datasets skewed toward certain cultures produce models that misunderstand accents, names, and local references
Prompt engineering — 'Act As a Role' technique; comparing Prompt v1 (generic) vs Prompt v2 (culturally situated role prompt) for persona generation
AI output variability — temperature settings and their effect on predictability vs creativity of model outputs
AI token prediction — how AI finds patterns in text to predict the next token, analogous to pattern recognition in design research
AI literacy MCQ questions on NPC dialogue consistency, output variability, and low-variability behaviour
Critical AI literacy — verifying and improving AI outputs rather than accepting them uncritically
learning_objectives: Identify your target players and create a clear persona and empathy map that show their goals, frustrations, motivations, and play patterns.
Use empathy insights and AI tools to explain and refine at least one game design decision, checking for bias and improving the player's experience (UX).
metadata.activity_type: mixed — pair work for persona creation and peer review, individual portfolio documentation and reflection
metadata.activity_description:
Starter – Design Starts with People: Students watch a clip from Steve Jobs at WWDC 1997 (0:30–3:00) on why customer experience must come before technology. They discuss how this applies to game design and complete a mini-task listing three specific frustrations and three specific motivations from games they personally play.

Activity 2.1 – Player Frustrations & Motivations (Assessment for Learning): Students identify specific frustrations and motivations that influence player engagement and enjoyment, recording three frustrations and three motivations from their own gaming experience.

Activity 2.2 – Persona Creation (Assessment for Learning): Using Bartle's Four Player Types framework, students create a detailed player persona including name, age, dominant player type, goals, frustrations, and desired mechanics. Students may optionally use AI to generate a draft persona, then critique it for bias, stereotypes, or missing cultural perspectives and document how they improved it.

Activity 2.3 – Empathy Map (Assessment for Learning): Students complete a Think / Feel / Say / Do empathy map for their target player, adding 3–5 motivation keywords at the centre. Students may use digital tools (Miro, FigJam, Canva) and paste a link. An AI bias check prompt is used to identify missing perspectives; students document how they improved their empathy map as a result.

Activity 2.4 – Empathy → Design Connection (Assessment for Learning): Students identify one concrete design decision informed by their persona and empathy map, writing 1–3 sentences explaining how empathy shaped the choice using provided sentence starters ('Because my player often struggles with…' / 'Since my player is motivated by…').

Portfolio Entry 2 – Player Experience (UX) (Assessment of Learning): Students upload screenshots of their persona (Activity 2.2) and empathy map (Activity 2.3), write their empathy-driven design decision (Activity 2.4), and clearly describe their target user.

Reflection – My Player's Experience (UX): Students complete the reflection statement ('My player is motivated by ___ and will stay engaged because my game will ___'), record an AI literacy reflection on how they used AI and what decisions they still made themselves, and self-assess against the lesson success criteria using an All/Most/Some format.

Extension – AI Mini-Lesson (optional): Students explore how AI bias originates from unrepresentative training data and discuss what an AI game assistant might misunderstand about UAE players. Students then apply the 'Act As a Role' prompting technique by running two versions of a persona prompt and comparing outputs for relevance, cultural detail, and usefulness.

Extension – Endstar Warm-Up (if time allows): Students choose between a Persona Builder Sprint (build a mini Endstar scene showing their player's personality or environment using 6 props max) or a Logic Loop Challenge (design one simple interaction reflecting their player's motivation, e.g. a door opens when a collectible is found).

Learning Check MCQs: Students complete three multiple-choice questions on AI literacy concepts (NPC dialogue consistency, AI output variability, low variability behaviour) in Classkick at the end of the lesson.

lessons.big_question: How can understanding our players help us design experiences they truly connect with?
lessons.uae_link: This lesson highlights the importance of understanding diverse users and communities, while considering cultural and social contexts relevant to the UAE.
success_criteria: Explain one design decision that meets player needs and motivations, and show how AI or feedback helped check for bias or missing insights.
Create a clear persona and empathy map showing what the player thinks, feels, says, and does — including their goals, frustrations, and motivations.
I created a clear persona and empathy map that shows what my player thinks, feels, says, and does.
I explained one design decision that meets my player's needs and motivations.
I used AI or feedback to refine my idea and reduce bias or missing insights.
curriculum_alignment: CSTA Computer Science Standards (US): 3A-AP-17 – Iterative refinement using feedback and testing. Students refine design decisions using empathy insights, peer discussion, and AI-supported reflection.
UK Computer Science: User-centred evaluation of digital solutions. Students evaluate how player needs and motivations influence digital design choices.
UK Design & Technology: KS3 1d – Understanding user needs. Students analyse user needs and contexts to inform design decisions before development.
IB Design: Criterion C – Creating the Solution (user focus). Students apply empathy and user research to inform justified design decisions.
IB Design: Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing. Linked to learning objectives and success criteria delivery.
Product Development Cycle – Empathise / Define Phase.

teacher_notes.notes: • The key question we're exploring today is how understanding our players can help us design experiences they truly connect with. • This collage of diverse players highlights the different motivations and playstyles we need to consider - the competitive player, the creative builder, the social gamer, and the explorer. • Deeply understanding our audience and what drives their engagement is critical to crafting experiences that resonate. • By putting the player at the center and designing with their needs and preferences in mind, we can create gaming worlds and mechanics that foster genuine connection. • This big picture perspective will guide our discussion as we dive into specific player insights and design strategies.
These are the grouped delivery objectives; retain the full 5-item list in the written plan.
This links directly to the Product Development Cycle and IB Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing. Links to IB Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing.
Teacher Note: Video link should be prepared and tested beforehand. Facilitate brief discussion emphasising empathy as a design skill. Collect mini-task responses to reference during persona creation.

Teacher Note: Provide persona templates. Students should create personas relevant to their chosen brief. AI-generated personas should be critiqued for bias, stereotypes, or missing cultural context. What assumptions did the AI make?

Teacher Note: Model completing one quadrant as a class. Emphasise that empathy maps should be based on research or genuine insight, not stereotypes. The AI prompt encourages students to question their own assumptions and consider accessibility.

This can create accidental bias (e.g., misunderstanding accents, misinterpreting cultural references). Mention empathy maps: "An empathy map helps humans design better experiences because we look at the whole person. AI doesn't do this. It only sees the data it was trained on."

Teacher Note (Exemplar Work Slide 8 – AI Extension Sample Responses): 1. Which setting made the AI most predictable? Sample Answer: "Low temperature (around 0.2) made the AI most predictable because the model chooses the safest, most likely next token and avoids creative or unusual options." 2. Which setting made the AI most creative or random? Sample Answer: "High temperature (around 1.0) made the AI more creative or random because the model is allowed to take riskier choices and pick words that are less common or less expected." 3. Why might a game studio or product team want to control randomness in an AI model? Sample Answer: "They may want predictable behaviour for things like tutorials, NPC dialogue, safety, or consistency. Too much randomness can break the experience or confuse players." 4. How is your work from last lesson similar to how an AI predicts the next token? Sample Answer: "Just like we looked for patterns and structured information in the design brief, the AI also looks for patterns in the text to decide what comes next. Both processes use clues from earlier information to make the best prediction."

metadata.activity_description:
Starter – Human vs Digital Discovery: Students pair up to compare the strengths and limitations of human conversation versus AI-based research. Each pair shares one breakthrough moment from human conversation and one from digital research, then together identifies what made each method powerful in that situation.

Portfolio Entry 3 – Research Comparison Table (7 min, Assessment of Learning): Students investigate their game's real-world setting or experience using three research types — Primary (Human: interviews, observations, user testing), Secondary (books, articles, videos, credible web sources), and AI-Based (Gemini, ChatGPT). For each type, students record one research finding that could affect gameplay or puzzles, one strength, and one limitation. Students reflect on which research type gave them the most useful ideas. An exemplar is provided showing a Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque game brief with completed entries, e.g. a light-restoring puzzle inspired by how the mosque's light changes from golden in the morning to blue and purple at night.

Activity 3.1 – Research Sprint Notes (5 min, Assessment for Learning): Using their chosen game brief, students complete a table for Human Research and AI Research, recording one insight gained, one limitation discovered, and the connection to their game brief. Optional screenshots of interview notes, AI prompts/responses, or reference images can be added.

Explore: Brief Immersion – Students become 'research detectives' exploring three research types through structured parallel tasks. Human Research involves interviewing a partner about the real-world setting of their game brief (what the place looks and feels like, how people move through it, what challenges exist). AI Research involves using AI tools to investigate the same setting (how similar real-world places function, cultural/historical rules, environmental details).

Analyse: Keyword & Pattern Extraction – Working in pairs, students conduct a rapid comparison of human vs AI research methods. Students ask follow-up questions about what a place feels like to explore, how people interact with it, and what challenges naturally exist. UAE example discussed: at GITEX Global 2025, Sheikh Tahnoon bin Zayed Al Nahyan designed his Abu Dhabi residence using 500 ChatGPT prompts in collaboration with architectural firm Nikken Sekkei, illustrating how AI supports creative design decisions while still relying on human expertise and judgement.

Deepen: Endstar Exploration Challenge – Students apply research insights in Endstar by choosing ONE interaction challenge: Option A (Trigger-Based Interaction using lever, pressure plate, or switch) or Option B (Information-Based Interaction using NPC dialogue, signpost, audio log, or environmental clue). Students take a screenshot as portfolio evidence and caption it with a description and link to their brief.

Portfolio Entry 4 – Endstar Prototype Evidence: Students upload a screenshot or short clip of their completed Endstar interaction, noting how it applies their research findings.

Reflect: My Next Discovery (5 min): Students complete the sentence: 'The most useful research method was ___ because it helped me ___; next I'll explore ___ to improve my game in Endstar.' Students also complete an AI Literacy Reflection with sentence starters: 'Today AI helped me by…' and 'I still needed to decide…'

Self-Assessment – My Success Criteria: Students assess against three levels — All: completed comparison table and 1 Endstar mini-challenge; Most: compared two sources and recorded limitations; Some: analysed deeper research issues (e.g. AI bias, reliability, cultural assumptions) and linked them to brief.

Learning Check – MCQ 3.1–3.3: Three multiple choice questions on AI Literacy covering prompt constraints, role assignment in prompts, and reproducible prompting. Completed on Classkick. Answer keys: MCQ 3.1 = B, MCQ 3.2 = B, MCQ 3.3 = C.

Extension Activities (optional, 10 min each): 1) AI Mini-Lesson – Think–Pair–Share: 'What Can AI Not See at Sheikh Zayed Mosque?' — students identify experiential gaps such as temperature of marble floors, echoes of the adhan, spiritual feeling, and scale. 2) Design Constraint activity: students discuss design problems that only emerge when visiting the real place (scale, light direction, movement etiquette). 3) Debug Rescue Lite: fix a broken rule chain in Endstar to restore functionality, practising troubleshooting skills.

headers: One research finding about your game’s setting, experience, or puzzles (what you discovered and how it could affect gameplay)
rows: Primary (Human)
Interviews, observations, user testing

My brother told me that the light in Sheikh Zayed Mosque changes from golden in the morning to blue and purple at night. I can use this idea to make a puzzle where the player fixes lights to match different times of day.
You get real emotions and personal details that websites don't have.
My brother couldn't remember exact facts like how many columns there are.
Secondary
Books, articles, videos, credible web sources, google search
I found out the mosque has 82 domes and reflecting pools in the courtyard. I could make a puzzle where the player uses the pools to reflect light into the main hall.
You get accurate facts and numbers from official sources.
It took a long time to find what I needed and some websites had different information.
AI-Based
Gemini, ChatGPT – AI summaries, pattern recognition
ChatGPT suggested puzzles using Islamic geometric patterns and gave me the idea to add an NPC guide who helps players and reminds them to be respectful.
It's really fast and gives you lots of ideas for brainstorming.
AI gives general mosque info, not specifically about Sheikh Zayed Mosque, and sometimes the facts are wrong.
Human Research
My classmate Ahmad said when he visited the mosque, the coolest part was walking through the huge courtyard and seeing the main dome appear slowly as you get closer.
Ahmad only visited once and he was more focused on taking photos than noticing small architectural details.
I can design my courtyard zone so the player slowly discovers the main hall, making the first view of the big dome feel like a special moment.
AI Research
I asked Gemini about mosque architecture and it explained that light and shadow are used on purpose in Islamic design to create a spiritual feeling.
Gemini gave general info about mosques but didn't know specific things about Sheikh Zayed Mosque like where the chandeliers are located.
My light-restoring puzzle makes more sense now because light isn't just decoration - it's actually an important part of the mosque's design and meaning.
🟨 Problem
Kind of
I know players need to restore the light, but I need to be more specific about WHY the light went out in the first place.
🟦 Audience
Yes
Players aged 13-16 who like calm puzzle games and learning about culture.
🟩 Purpose
Yes
To show how beautiful mosque architecture is and make players feel peaceful and amazed, like visiting the real place.
🩷 Constraints
Need to check
I know it has to be 5 minutes and use Endstar, but I need to remember: prayer area must stay private and blocked off.
🟧 Success Criteria
Not really

metadata.activity_description: The lesson begins with a Starter activity (From Information to Insight) where students identify one research insight from Lesson 3 that should influence their game design, share it with a partner, and explain why it matters. Activity 4.1 (How Game Objectives Work) has students analyse how game objectives guide player progress through three stages — Setup/Quest Giver, Core Challenge/Linked Mechanics, and Completion/Reward Event — then write one sentence per objective describing how their player will progress through their own game. Activity 4.2 (Rewrite the Brief as a Design Specification, 10 minutes) guides students to use their Lesson 1 brief and Lesson 3 research insights to rewrite their brief into a clear design specification with all five elements defined: Problem, Audience, Purpose, Constraints, and Success Criteria. An AI Assist prompt is provided: 'Rewrite this brief clearly for a 13-year-old in the UAE — keep purpose same, make goals measurable.' Students also study the R-E-X (Role, Example, Expectation) prompt engineering structure to make AI instructions clearer and more effective, practise identifying common prompt mistakes (too vague, no context, no format), and create improved structured prompts. Portfolio Entry 5 (Rewritten Brief) requires students to submit their rewritten brief using all five elements and explain what changed from their Lesson 1 brief. Portfolio Entry 6 (Game Design Concept Board) asks students to gather 3–6 visuals representing their game concept — sketches, object references, environment images, mechanic diagrams, or storyboards — and upload them with captions explaining how each element defines their game idea. Activity 4.3 (Team Charter, 5 minutes) has students form balanced teams of 3–4 with defined roles (Designer, Programmer, Art Lead, Story/Marketing), document responsibilities and personal strengths, and agree on 1–2 team commitments. A Peer Review stage has students exchange briefs and evaluate each other using a checklist covering problem specificity, audience definition, UAE context connection, constraint realism, and measurable success criteria. The Reflection/Plenary (My Project Vision) asks students to articulate their vision using the sentence frame 'My game aims to ___ because ___; we'll know it's successful when ___' and complete an AI Literacy Reflection using sentence starters. The lesson includes three optional Extension/Adaptive Tasks: an AI Mini-Lesson on what AI cannot sense or feel at a UAE heritage site (Think-Pair-Share), an AI Mini-Lesson on design constraints that only emerge from real-world visits to physical spaces, and an Endstar Warm-Up Logic Blueprint connecting brief-writing to practical mechanic implementation. Three AI Literacy Learning Check MCQs cover prompt framing, user skill impact on AI output quality, and how constraints improve output reproducibility. The source also includes exemplar student work showing completed versions of Portfolio Entry 3 (Research Comparison Table covering primary, secondary, and AI-based research about Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque) and Activity 3.1 (Research Sprint Notes), which model the expected depth of research and reflection.

teacher_notes.notes: Teacher Note: Model one row together (e.g., 'Heritage Puzzle Game'). Remind students this research supports their Lesson 1 chosen brief. Encourage complete sentences and specific examples. Link back to the Term 1 idea of 'inputs and outputs.' Ensure every group records at least one bias in the AI column. This key shows which industry disciplines are applied in each task throughout Lessons 3–6.
Teacher Note: Ensure students include both human and AI research examples. Encourage short captions. Remind students that file titles should include their name and game brief.

Teacher Note: Encourage students to be honest about their preferences while recognizing that different research methods serve different purposes. Some may prefer human interaction, others may appreciate AI efficiency. Both are valid.

Teacher Notes (after students answer): Only after they share, you summarise: AI produces descriptions based on text patterns. But it can't stand in the courtyard, feel the heat, hear echoes, smell incense, see light bounce off marble. This creates gaps when analysing a UAE brief.

Teacher Notes (explanation AFTER they guess): Explain that these are design constraints AI cannot reveal: scale, mood, movement patterns, environmental details, light direction. This is why briefs often require primary research.

slides.content:
‹#›

Term 2

Lesson 4

Lesson 4: "Rewriting the Brief: From Insights to Action" — Define Phase

‹#›

Today's lesson focuses on the Define phase of the Product Development Cycle. 
We'll transform the research insights from Lesson 3 into clear, actionable project goals that will guide our game development.

UAE Link: This lesson reflects the importance of clear problem definition by refining ideas into focused, actionable design goals within a UAE context.
Reflection Question: Why is clearly defining a problem important before developing digital solutions?

Big Question / Big Picture

Game Design

‹#›

How do designers turn research into clear project goals?

Lesson 4: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Form Teams with Clear Roles
Form balanced teams with clear roles and timelines to support the design process.

Combine Research into a Brief
Use human and AI research

headers: One research finding about your game’s setting, experience, or puzzles (what you discovered and how it could affect gameplay)
rows: Primary (Human)
Interviews, observations, user testing

My brother told me that the light in Sheikh Zayed Mosque changes from golden in the morning to blue and purple at night. I can use this idea to make a puzzle where the player fixes lights to match different times of day.
You get real emotions and personal details that websites don't have.
My brother couldn't remember exact facts like how many columns there are.
Secondary
Books, articles, videos, credible web sources, google search
I found out the mosque has 82 domes and reflecting pools in the courtyard. I could make a puzzle where the player uses the pools to reflect light into the main hall.
You get accurate facts and numbers from official sources.
It took a long time to find what I needed and some websites had different information.
AI-Based
Gemini, ChatGPT – AI summaries, pattern recognition
ChatGPT suggested puzzles using Islamic geometric patterns and gave me the idea to add an NPC guide who helps players and reminds them to be respectful.
It's really fast and gives you lots of ideas for brainstorming.
AI gives general mosque info, not specifically about Sheikh Zayed Mosque, and sometimes the facts are wrong.
Human Research
My classmate Ahmad said when he visited the mosque, the coolest part was walking through the huge courtyard and seeing the main dome appear slowly as you get closer.
Ahmad only visited once and he was more focused on taking photos than noticing small architectural details.
I can design my courtyard zone so the player slowly discovers the main hall, making the first view of the big dome feel like a special moment.
AI Research
I asked Gemini about mosque architecture and it explained that light and shadow are used on purpose in Islamic design to create a spiritual feeling.
Gemini gave general info about mosques but didn't know specific things about Sheikh Zayed Mosque like where the chandeliers are located.
My light-restoring puzzle makes more sense now because light isn't just decoration - it's actually an important part of the mosque's design and meaning.
🟨 Problem
Kind of
I know players need to restore the light, but I need to be more specific about WHY the light went out in the first place.
🟦 Audience
Yes
Players aged 13-16 who like calm puzzle games and learning about culture.
🟩 Purpose
Yes
To show how beautiful mosque architecture is and make players feel peaceful and amazed, like visiting the real place.
🩷 Constraints
Need to check
I know it has to be 5 minutes and use Endstar, but I need to remember: prayer area must stay private and blocked off.
🟧 Success Criteria
Not really

I wrote "restore the light" but I need to be clearer - like how many puzzles? What happens at the end exactly?  I need to make my Success Criteria more specific. Like "player completes 2 light puzzles and sees the final chandelier turn on" instead of just "restore the light."

lessons.lesson_title: Lesson 5: Idea Generation & Brainstorming – From Concept to Micro-Prototype

metadata.title: Idea Generation & Brainstorming – From Concept to Micro-Prototype
core_topics: Idea generation and brainstorming for game design
Game concept development: core mechanic, player goal, and reward loop
Product Development Cycle – Ideate → Prototype Phase
AI-assisted 3D concept iteration (Nano Banana and Meshy AI)
AI literacy: training data bias and AI capabilities and limits
3D asset creation pipeline: 2D concept to 3D model to game engine
ai_focus: AI as creative co-pilot for rapid 3D concept iteration using Nano Banana and Meshy AI workflow
Text-to-Image AI for character and environment concept sketching (Nano Banana)
Image-to-3D AI conversion and PBR texture generation using Meshy AI
AI training data bias: AI reflects patterns dominant in its training data and human-labelled examples (OECD Knowledge Area K2, Code K2.1)
AI capabilities and limits: AI lacks contextual understanding, judgement, and real-world constraints such as game engine performance requirements (OECD Knowledge Area K4, Code K4.1)
Bias in AI-generated content: risk of reinforcing dominant cultural or market assumptions when AI-generated designs are selected without questioning representation (OECD K4.1)
AI Literacy Reflection: evaluating and critiquing AI output rather than trusting it uncritically; using own judgement to make decisions
Transformer fundamentals: next-token prediction and probabilistic outputs leading to variable results for the same prompt (PD session context)
learning_objectives: Generate and Refine Concepts: Create and refine three game concepts linked to your brief using sketches or storyboards.
Translate to Micro-Prototype: Develop one concept into a micro-prototype in Endstar and use peer feedback to plan improvements.
metadata.activity_type: mixed (individual concept generation and prototype build with pair peer feedback and whole class discussion)
metadata.activity_description:
Starter (whole class discussion): Students identify one simple game mechanic they enjoy and explain why it feels engaging. Discussion questions explore what makes an idea playable and how to bridge the gap between imagination and interactive experience.

Mini Recap – What Makes a Great Game?: A brief recap of the three essential pillars introduced in Term 1 — Mechanics (what the player does), Design/Dynamics (how the game feels), and Narrative/Theme (why the game matters) — to guide game-building in this lesson.

Explore – Activity 5.1 – Concept Generation (Assessment for Learning): Students generate three game concepts based on their brief using paper, Miro, Figma, Canva, Google Sketch, or a tablet drawing app. For each concept they identify: Core Mechanic (main player action — collect, build, solve, explore), Player Goal (what success looks like and how players know they're progressing), and Reward Loop (Action → Feedback → Motivation). Each concept is documented on a concept board with sketches or storyboards and uploaded or linked. An AI prompt is provided: 'Suggest two mechanics that fit a game about heritage or discovery.'

Explore – Activity 5.2 – Select & Plan (5 minutes, Assessment for Learning): Students review their three concepts and choose ONE to take forward into their micro-prototype. They discuss with a partner using three questions: Which concept feels most engaging for players? What might be challenging to build in Endstar? How does this concept connect to your brief's purpose? Students then list the Endstar tools they expect to use: dialogue systems for NPCs, triggers and interactions, props and environment pieces, logic chains for mechanics.

Deepen – Micro-Prototype Build: Students build a tiny scene in Endstar showing the main mechanic or interaction, following a four-step process: (1) Set Up Scene — create basic environment with essential props; (2) Add Interaction — implement one key mechanic (button, dialogue, trigger); (3) Test & Debug — play through the interaction and fix any issues; (4) Document Progress — take screenshots and note ideas for improvement. A Vimeo video recap ('How to Build a Basic Puzzle in Endstar') is linked for reference.

Portfolio Entry 7 – Micro-Prototype Evidence (Assessment of Learning): Students upload concept sketches, storyboard, or Endstar screenshot and complete a prototype feature table (Prototype Feature / Description/Caption / Link to Brief). They write a brief reflection: 'One thing that worked in my prototype was ___; one thing I'll improve next is ___.' Additional optional upload space is provided for extra sketches or references.

Reflect – My Prototype Journey: Students reflect on their journey from concept to working prototype, considering what surprised them and what they learned about their own design approach. They complete AI Literacy Reflection sentence starters: 'Today AI helped me by…' and 'I still needed to decide…'

Self-Assessment – My Success Criteria: Students identify their achievement level — All (created three concepts and chose one to prototype), Most (refined concepts and built a micro-prototype with one working action), Some (improved prototype further with extra mechanics or polish).

Extension / Adaptive Task – Mechanic Remix (if time allows): Students take an existing Endstar scene and add one new interaction or modify an existing mechanic to apply iterative thinking and rapid prototyping skills.

Stretch Activity – AI Workflow: 3D Iteration with Nano Banana & Meshy AI (optional, for advanced students): (1) Create a basic prop or environment blockout in Nano Banana using simple geometric shapes; (2) Take a screenshot and upload to Meshy AI; (3) Use Meshy AI's Image-to-3D or Text+Image features to enhance the concept; (4) Compare original blockout with AI-refined concept and write a caption reflection. Slides also cover a detailed workflow for AI-assisted character concept art: inputting descriptive prompts into a text-to-image tool, editing the image for a clean background, uploading to Meshy AI to generate an untextured base 3D model, then applying PBR-mapped textures. A final slide contextualises the full industry pipeline: Meshy AI → Blender (retopology and rigging) → Endstar game engine.

headers: Why it Worked / What I learned
rows: Player spawned facing away from the NPC caretaker, so they walked the wrong direction and missed the dialogue completely
Moved the Trigger Volume to cover the entire spawn area and rotated the spawn point to face the NPC
Now players trigger the dialogue automatically when they spawn - I learned spawn orientation matters a lot for first impressions
Only 2 of the 3 crystals were counting. Counter showed 2/3 even after collecting all crystals
Found that Crystal #3 wasn't wired to the Counter Rule - I forgot to click "Confirm" on the wire
Always check every wire individually and click Confirm! One missing connection breaks the whole system
Door opened but there was no feedback - player didn't know it happened because they were far away collecting the last crystal
Players need feedback at the moment something happens, not just the result. Sound or text helps a lot
Added wire from Counter Rule OnCountReached → Billboard SetRawText to display "Door Unlocked!" message

metadata.title: Prototype v1 – Building the Core Mechanic
core_topics: Iterative prototyping and the Product Development Cycle (Prototype ↔ Test ↔ Improve)
Input → Process → Output model in game systems
Endstar game development tools and logic
Reward loops and player experience design
AI-assisted debugging with Phind AI and chain-of-thought prompting
AI literacy: AI limitations and human judgement
ai_focus: Phind AI – AI tool for technical reasoning and debugging; students use chain-of-thought prompting to identify causes and evaluate multiple fixes
Self-Consistency Prompting for Debugging: asking AI to show reasoning and check its own logic, then comparing response quality against a general prompt
AI Literacy MCQ 6.1: AI is poorly suited to final design decisions because it lacks lived experience, values, and judgement (OECD K1.1)
AI Literacy MCQ 6.2: Human evaluation grounded in real constraints should guide final decisions when AI confident output conflicts with player feedback (OECD K3.3)
AI Literacy MCQ 6.3: Incorrect facts delivered confidently illustrate an AI limitation (hallucination) rather than a prompt failure (OECD K4.3)
AI Literacy Reflection: students document how AI helped, what they still decided themselves, and whether they checked AI output before trusting it
Transformer fundamentals (PD): tokens, next-token prediction, probabilistic outputs, same prompt can produce different answers
Nano Banana → Meshy AI workflow for rapid 3D asset iteration from blockout to refined asset (PD)
Structured prompting: adding constraints, format specifications, and role prompts to improve AI outputs (PD preview – covered in detail 2 February)
learning_objectives: Develop your Lesson 5 micro-prototype into a functional Prototype v1 that meets brief requirements.
Implement and debug core mechanics through peer and AI-assisted testing to ensure functionality.
metadata.activity_description:
Lesson 6 opens with a Starter activity in which students review their micro-prototype from Lesson 5, recapping reward loops and identifying one improvement to carry forward into Prototype v1.

Activity 6.1 – Prototype Planning (Assessment for Learning): Students map out their Prototype v1 by reviewing what worked and what broke in their micro-prototype, planning the build order and identifying the Endstar tools required, listing 2–3 essential core features, and predicting potential player issues using an AI prompt ('Predict three issues a player might have with this prototype'). Students also sketch a flowchart showing the Input → Process → Output sequence for one mechanic from their prototype, using digital tools such as Miro, Figma, Canva, or diagrams.net, or on paper.

Activity 6.2 – Build Prototype v1 (Assessment for Learning): Students transform their micro-prototype into a more complete experience by expanding the core mechanic, connecting elements with logic chains and triggers in Endstar, and testing as they build. The Product Development Cycle's Prototype ↔ Test ↔ Improve loop is introduced as the working framework. A UAE context example (Careem) illustrates iterative digital product development.

Activity 6.3 – Playtest & Debug (Assessment for Learning): Students playtest their Prototype v1 from start to finish, identifying anything confusing, broken, or missing. They may use Phind AI with chain-of-thought prompting to assist debugging after attempting fixes themselves. Students fix one issue at a time, retest after each fix, and document findings in the Playtest & Debug Log table (screenshot, issue found, fix applied, what they learned). If no bugs are found, they must document one playtest observation and one planned improvement.

Portfolio Entry 8 – Prototype v1 Evidence (Assessment of Learning): Students upload screenshots or gameplay footage of Prototype v1 (via Drive, OneDrive, or Endstar link) and complete the sentence 'What worked well in my first build was ___; what I'll improve next is ___'. Optional on-image labels can be added.

Reflection / Plenary: Students complete the 'My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment' (All/Most/Some differentiation), an AI Literacy Reflection with sentence starters ('Today AI helped me by…', 'I still needed to decide…'), and three AI Literacy MCQ exit-ticket questions on Classkick.

Extension – AI Mini-Lesson: Self-Consistency Prompting for Debugging — students compare a general debug prompt versus a self-consistency prompt and evaluate the quality of AI responses.

Extension – Endstar Warm-Up: Quick Fix Challenge — students find and fix a broken scene in Endstar, practising systematic debugging.
artifacts: Flowchart sketch showing Input → Process → Output for one prototype mechanic – Activity 6.1
Prototype v1 game build in Endstar – Activity 6.2
Playtest & Debug Log table documenting issues, fixes, and learning – Activity 6.3
Portfolio Entry 8 – Prototype v1 screenshots or gameplay footage with written reflection
AI Literacy Reflection (sentence starters)
assessment_signals: Assessment for Learning: Activities 6.1, 6.2, 6.3 (formative, ongoing)
Assessment of Learning: Portfolio Entry 8 – Prototype v1 Evidence (upload screenshots or gameplay footage; explain one issue identified and how it was resolved)
AI MCQ exit-ticket questions on Classkick (MCQ 6.1, 6.2, 6.3 – AI Literacy)
My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment (All/Most/Some differentiation)
Playtest & Debug Log documentation (Activity 6.2 table: issue found, fix applied, what I learned)
AI Literacy Reflection (sentence starters: 'Today AI helped me by…', 'I still needed to decide…')

lessons.detailed_activities:
Lesson 6 opens with a Starter activity in which students review their micro-prototype from Lesson 5, recapping reward loops and identifying one improvement to carry forward into Prototype v1.

Activity 6.1 – Prototype Planning (Assessment for Learning): Students map out their Prototype v1 by reviewing what worked and what broke in their micro-prototype, planning the build order and identifying the Endstar tools required, listing 2–3 essential core features, and predicting potential player issues using an AI prompt ('Predict three issues a player might have with this prototype'). Students also sketch a flowchart showing the Input → Process → Output sequence for one mechanic from their prototype, using digital tools such as Miro, Figma, Canva, or diagrams.net, or on paper.

Activity 6.2 – Build Prototype v1 (Assessment for Learning): Students transform their micro-prototype into a more complete experience by expanding the core mechanic, connecting elements with logic chains and triggers in Endstar, and testing as they build. The Product Development Cycle's Prototype ↔ Test ↔ Improve loop is introduced as the working framework. A UAE context example (Careem) illustrates iterative digital product development.

Activity 6.3 – Playtest & Debug (Assessment for Learning): Students playtest their Prototype v1 from start to finish, identifying anything confusing, broken, or missing. They may use Phind AI with chain-of-thought prompting to assist debugging after attempting fixes themselves. Students fix one issue at a time, retest after each fix, and document findings in the Playtest & Debug Log table (screenshot, issue found, fix applied, what they learned). If no bugs are found, they must document one playtest observation and one planned improvement.

Portfolio Entry 8 – Prototype v1 Evidence (Assessment of Learning): Students upload screenshots or gameplay footage of Prototype v1 (via Drive, OneDrive, or Endstar link) and complete the sentence 'What worked well in my first build was ___; what I'll improve next is ___'. Optional on-image labels can be added.

Reflection / Plenary: Students complete the 'My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment' (All/Most/Some differentiation), an AI Literacy Reflection with sentence starters ('Today AI helped me by…', 'I still needed to decide…'), and three AI Literacy MCQ exit-ticket questions on Classkick.

Extension – AI Mini-Lesson: Self-Consistency Prompting for Debugging — students compare a general debug prompt versus a self-consistency prompt and evaluate the quality of AI responses.

Extension – Endstar Warm-Up: Quick Fix Challenge — students find and fix a broken scene in Endstar, practising systematic debugging.

Expanding gameplay mechanics and connecting Rule Blocks
Immersion enhancement through visuals, sound, dialogue, and environment
Comic strip snapshot as a design analysis tool
AI literacy: evaluating AI outputs and understanding AI confidence
ai_focus: Using AI (Claude AI, Gemini, ChatGPT) to analyse game prototypes for flow and immersion issues via uploaded screenshots
Generating AI prompts for mechanic expansion: 'Suggest an interaction that encourages exploration for my [game theme]. The interaction should connect two simple mechanics and reward curious players.'
AI-assisted debugging: applying AI suggestions to fix prototype issues and documenting results
Claude AI (Anthropic) introduced as a new tool for interpreting tone, context, and emotion in storytelling and world-building
Using AI to summarise and theme peer tester comments (AI Analysis Documentation)
RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) — Retrieve, Augment, Generate pipeline for maintaining accuracy in educational content
AI hallucination and how RAG reduces it by grounding responses in retrieved information

AI MCQ K4.3: Fluent or confident AI outputs may still be misleading or incorrect
AI Literacy Reflection: students reflect on how they used AI, what they still had to decide themselves, and how they checked AI output

metadata.activity_description:
Activity 7.1 – Comic Strip Snapshot (Story or Gameplay): Students quickly create a 6-panel comic strip showing key scenes or gameplay moments from their game. Students sketch simple drawings or upload images, adding short notes or dialogue to show what is happening. The six panels should show the main flow of the game — either story or gameplay progression. After completing the comic strip, students use AI tools (ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude) by uploading screenshots to identify flow and immersion issues, receiving a checklist for both flow and immersion.

Activity 7.2 – Immersion Ideas: Students use their comic strip to identify where immersion breaks or could be improved. They fill in an Immersion Ideas Table listing specific ideas (e.g., lighting, sound, color, dialogue) and explain why each would enhance immersion. Students upload visual references that inspired their ideas, then share ideas with a partner to discuss which would have the biggest impact on player engagement.

Activity 7.3 – Expanding Mechanics: Students choose one expansion option: connect two existing mechanics (e.g., button opens door, door reveals key), add a timer element to create urgency, or create a multi-step sequence requiring multiple actions. They fill in a Planning Table documenting Mechanic 1, Mechanic 2, how they connect, and any AI insight used. A teacher demonstration video on connecting two Rule Blocks is provided, along with an AI support prompt: 'Suggest an interaction that encourages exploration for my [game theme]. The interaction should connect two simple mechanics and reward curious players.'

Activity 7.4 – Internal Playtesting & Feedback: Students exchange devices with a partner and play through the prototype completely, evaluating gameplay flow and immersion. Observations are recorded using WWW (What Went Well) and EBI (Even Better If) format, including immersion feedback, bugs found, AI fixes applied, and results. Students also document any AI debugging support used: the problem, which AI suggestion was tried, and whether it solved the issue.

Activity 7.5 – Design Enhancement: Students choose one area to focus on (audio, visual, dialogue, or environment). They apply an AI suggestion using the provided prompt template, test the change, and document the improvement in an Enhancement Documentation Table showing the scene improved, AI suggestion, and what was changed and why. Reflection questions ask which enhancement had the biggest impact, how Lesson 2 persona research influenced choices, and what was learned about technical implementation and emotional impact.

Portfolio Entry 9a – Prototype v2 Expanded Mechanics: Students upload evidence (screenshot of linked mechanics, gameplay clip, or short video) and write a reflection: 'This mechanic improves gameplay by…'

Portfolio Entry 9b – Prototype V2 Immersion Enhancement: Students upload evidence of visual, audio, lighting, or dialogue improvements and complete the sentence: 'This immersion change improves player engagement by ______ because ______'

Stretch Activity – Cutscene Challenge: Students who finish early add a short cutscene using Endstar tools such as camera changes, dialogue nodes, timed events, or animated props. Examples include trigger-activated camera pans revealing new areas, NPC dialogue scenes introducing challenges or clues, fade-in/fade-out moments before major events, timed lighting or sound cues, and intro sequences when players enter new zones. Students capture a screenshot or short clip of the cutscene captioned 'Cutscene created to enhance player immersion.'

Reflection / Plenary: Students reflect on their journey from Prototype V1 to V2, completing sentence starters about their most engaging moment and biggest enhancement. Students complete My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment and an AI Literacy Reflection on how AI helped and what decisions they still made themselves. Students then answer AI MCQ exit-ticket questions on Classkick covering RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation), evaluating multiple AI outputs, and AI confidence vs. correctness.

metadata.title: Peer Testing & Feedback Analysis
core_topics: Peer testing and external feedback collection
AI-assisted feedback analysis using Claude and Gemini
AI literacy: evaluating AI output quality
AI output variability and probabilistic inference
ai_focus: Using Claude or Gemini to summarise and rank tester feedback themes
AI prompt engineering for feedback analysis: 'Summarise the main themes from this game testing feedback and rank them by importance. Here is the feedback: [paste all comments here].'

AI planning prompt for generating evidence-based next steps: 'Suggest 2 specific changes I could make to my game based on this feedback, keeping within the scope of Endstar's capabilities. The main issues are: [list your top 3 themes]'
Comparing AI tools: Claude and Gemini recommended over ChatGPT for grouping tester comments, finding recurring issues, and ranking problems by importance
Evaluating AI outputs for accuracy, relevance, bias, and clarity
AI literacy: probabilistic inference and how explicit constraints improve output reproducibility
AI output variability controlled by temperature and sampling parameters
Human structuring of AI inputs shapes system outputs
AI Literacy Reflection: identifying when AI helped vs when human judgement was still required
learning_objectives: Analyze tester feedback to identify key patterns and prioritise improvements.
Carry out structured external peer testing to gather WWW/EBI feedback.
metadata.activity_type: mixed — pair peer testing circuits with individual analysis and group theme mapping
metadata.activity_description:
Lesson 8 begins with a Starter on feedback quality (Slide 6): students discuss the difference between helpful and unhelpful feedback, then attempt a Feedback Rewrite Challenge — improving a poor feedback comment ('The game is boring and confusing.') by identifying exactly which part was boring or confusing and suggesting a concrete improvement.

Activity 8.1 – Feedback Rewrite: Students complete a Feedback Comparison Table by rewriting the poor feedback comment twice with improved versions, explaining what made each version better and why it helps the developer. Reflection question: What are three key elements that make feedback truly useful for game developers?

Explore: Peer Testing Circuits with AI Support (Slide 8): A Quick-Fix Sprint (5 minutes) asks students to fix one small issue in Endstar before formal feedback analysis begins. Students then form pairs, set a 5–7 minute testing timer, play through the prototype completely, fill out WWW/EBI feedback forms (Activity 8.2), switch roles, and collect feedback from at least 3 different testers. Students use Claude or Gemini with a structured summarisation prompt to analyse all collected feedback and paste the AI summary into Activity 8.2. The slide also explains why Claude/Gemini are recommended over ChatGPT for grouping and ranking feedback themes.

Activity 8.2 – Peer Testing Results (Slide 9): Each tester completes one row in a Feedback Collection Table recording What Went Well (WWW), Even Better If (EBI), and specific issues found. Students document their AI feedback analysis summary and identify the top 3 recurring themes.

Analyse: Theme Mapping & Next Steps (Slide 10): Students create sticky notes (physical or digital via Miro) for each piece of feedback, group similar feedback together, label each group with a theme name, and prioritise themes by frequency and impact. An AI planning prompt is used to generate 2 specific actionable next steps within Endstar's capabilities based on the top 3 themes.

Portfolio Entry 10a – Theme Mapping: Students upload their theme map from Miro or a photo of physical sticky note groupings.

Portfolio Entry 10b – Theme Mapping & Planning: Students complete a Theme Priority Table ranking feedback themes by frequency, impact level, and priority. They document AI-suggested changes with rationale and plan which changes to implement in Lesson 9.

Optional Stretch Activity – Easter Egg Challenge: Students who finish early add a hidden feature to their game inspired by tester feedback (e.g., hidden trigger block opening a new path, reward prop appearing after a specific action, secret sound effect or dialogue, colour or lighting change rewarding discovery). Two YouTube video references are provided as inspiration.

Reflect: My Development Process (Slide 15): Students reflect on how theme mapping and priority decisions helped them choose the most impactful next steps and complete AI Literacy Reflection sentence starters, plus self-assess their AI use.

Extension/Adaptive Task (Slide 17): Students create a scorecard rating their AI feedback analysis on four criteria — Accuracy, Relevance, Bias, and Clarity (scale 1–4 each) — to develop critical evaluation skills for AI outputs.

Endless Studios Session (Slide 19): Open studio time to build, experiment, test with the team, fix bugs, try new ideas, and explore AI tools in Endstar.

‹#›

Term 2

Lesson 8

Lesson 8: Peer Testing & Feedback Analysis

‹#›

In this stage, students conduct structured peer tests, gather data, identify patterns, and use AI to summarise and visualise feedback for evidence-based design improvements.

This stage mirrors real-world game development, where designers test their prototypes with players outside their core team to gather unbiased, customer-like insights.

Product Development Cycle – Term 2 | Prototype → Test & Evaluate Phase

“Unlike Lesson 7’s internal testing, this lesson focuses on feedback from peers outside your team to simulate real player testing.”

UAE Link: This lesson reflects a culture of continuous improvement and collaboration in the UAE by using peer feedback and testing to inform better design decisions.
Reflection Question: How can peer feedback help improve the effectiveness of a digital solution?

Big Question / Big Picture

metadata.title: Term 2 – Lesson 9-10
core_topics: Trigger Volumes and Dialogue Node wiring in Endstar
Project timeline planning with milestone tracking
ai_focus: Using AI suggestions for Endstar wiring patterns (Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node)
AI analysis of before-and-after prototype versions for player experience evaluation
Documenting AI suggestions used during implementation in the portfolio log
Evaluating AI output: assessing whether guidance is clear, actionable, and matches Endstar workflow
Endstar AI Chatbot as a classroom support tool for step-by-step guidance, troubleshooting, and task clarification for both teachers and students
Identifying when AI output is missing information or misleading
metadata.activity_description:
Activity 9.1 – Impact vs Effort Action Planning: Students use their three improvements from Portfolio Task 10 (Lesson 8) and place each idea in an Impact vs Effort matrix (High/Medium/Low impact vs Low/Medium/High effort). They select the top 2 changes to implement today based on the matrix positioning.

Portfolio Entry 11a – Prototype V3 Refinement Implementation: Students upload before-and-after screenshots showing the improvements they made to their Endstar game prototype, labelling each screenshot (A, B, C, D, etc.) and describing what changed, the wiring used (e.g. Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node, OnTriggered → GiveInstruction), and the outcome.

Portfolio Entry 11b – Prototype V3 Implementation Log: Students document each change in a table (change made, before/after screenshot references, AI suggestion used, impact assessment, next steps) and answer three reflection questions about which change had the most impact, how AI suggestions helped or hindered the process, and what surprised them.

Activity 9.2 – Impact Evaluation: Students evaluate the effectiveness of their changes using an Impact Assessment Table cross-referencing original tester feedback, the change made, whether it addresses the feedback (Y/N), any new issues created, and an overall impact rating (1–5). Students paste their AI evaluation response and complete a Validation Checklist.

Activity 10.1 – Team Roles Table (10 minutes): Students include their new Project Manager and update each teammate's role, responsibilities, strengths, and tasks for the next milestone in a team roles table.

Activity 10.2 – Project Timeline Table: Students refine their project timeline for Week 5–6 (Term 2) and Weeks 1–2 (Term 3), specifying milestones, dates, tasks, owners, risks, and contingency plans.

Activity 10.3 – Risk & Response Table (15 minutes): Students document at least five potential project risks (including at least one project-specific risk), their impact level (High/Medium/Low), likelihood (High/Medium/Low), and a response plan.

metadata.title: Team Roles & Milestones — Collaborative Project Planning
core_topics: Project Manager role in game development
ai_focus: Chain-of-Thought prompting and responsible use
AI reasoning transparency and intermediate reasoning steps
Evaluating AI outputs rather than accepting them uncritically
Role-specific AI prompt design (Designer, Programmer, Art Lead, Project Manager)
AI suggestions in game refinement (Trigger Volume wiring, NPC placement)
AI Literacy Reflection practice
OECD AI competency framework (K1, K3, K4)
Gemini as an AI tool reference
learning_objectives: Clarify and strengthen team roles, responsibilities, and shared accountability in your game development project.
Refine your shared project timeline by mapping milestones, risks, owners, and next steps for your team.
metadata.activity_description:
The lesson opens with a Starter activity (1–2 minutes) where students discuss with a partner which product-team skills their group already does well and which they need to strengthen, framed around Andrew Ng's perspective on product decision-making in real game studios.

Activity 10.1 – Team Roles Table (group, ~10 minutes): Students update their team roles table to include a newly assigned Project Manager role. Each team member records their role, responsibilities, strengths, and tasks for the next week. The table is designed to ensure balanced workload distribution and clear accountability across Designer, Programmer, Art Lead, and Story/Marketing Lead roles, with the Project Manager added on top of an existing role.

Activity 10.2 – Project Timeline Table (group): Students refine their project timeline covering Week 5–6 of Term 2 and Weeks 1–2 of Term 3. For each milestone they specify the date, tasks, owner, risks, and contingency plans, with emphasis on identifying potential bottlenecks and task dependencies.

Activity 10.3 – Risk & Response Table (group, 15 minutes): Students document at least five potential project risks — including team member absence, technical difficulties, scope creep, and time constraints — plus at least one project-specific risk. For each risk they assess impact level (High/Medium/Low) and likelihood (High/Medium/Low) and write a response plan.

Portfolio Entry 12 – Team Roles & Planning: Students upload screenshots or attach links for all three activity outputs (Team Roles Table, Project Timeline Table, Risk & Response Table) with a one-sentence reflection: 'Today I demonstrated…'

Reflect: Students write responses using provided sentence starters about their updated role and responsibilities, what the planning process revealed, and their team's most important risk. An AI Literacy Reflection (2–3 sentences) is also completed using sentence starters: 'Today AI helped me by…' and 'I still needed to decide…'

Self-Assessment (Slide 14): Students reflect against All/Most/Some differentiated success criteria.

Extension – AI Mini-Lesson: Each team role (Designer, Programmer, Art Lead, Project Manager) writes a role-specific AI prompt to support the project. Teams swap prompts and provide one feedback point. Evidence is added to the Team Charter.

Extension – Endstar Warm-Up: One student builds a small scene while another tests and logs bugs, followed by a discussion about how communication affects iteration.

The accompanying exemplar work document (Lessons 9–10) provides completed student examples including: Activity 9.1 – Impact vs Effort Action Planning matrix; Portfolio Entry 11a – Prototype V3 Refinement Implementation (before/after screenshots of Trigger Volume and NPC additions); Portfolio Entry 11b – Prototype V3 Implementation Log with AI suggestion documentation and reflection questions; and Activity 9.2 – Impact Evaluation with AI Analysis Documentation and a Validation Checklist.

metadata.title: Documentation & Portfolio Building — Telling the Design Story
core_topics: Design cycle evaluation (Empathise, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test & Evaluate)
ai_focus: AI Battle challenge comparing ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini
Prompt engineering for game development queries
Evaluating and comparing AI tool responses (rating 1–5, identifying strengths and weaknesses)
AI limitations and platform-specific knowledge (tools suggesting features not available in Endstar)
Using AI for NPC dialogue writing
Using AI for debugging wiring problems
Using AI for portfolio caption feedback
learning_objectives: Select and organise evidence from each stage of the Product Development Cycle.
Create clear captions and reflections to communicate the design process.
metadata.activity_description:
Starter – Portfolios That Speak: Students discuss what makes documentation clear or compelling and how designers communicate their design process effectively. Students complete the prompt: "My portfolio should show that I can ___."

Activity 11.1 – Portfolio Structure Review (Assessment for Learning): Students check they have at least one artefact for each required portfolio section.

Activity 11.2 – Curation & Captioning (Assessment for Learning): Students select their best 6–8 artefacts and write a one-sentence caption for each.

Portfolio Entry 13 – Curated Evidence & Captions (Assessment of Learning): Students upload 6–8 artefacts from Term 2 and write one clear caption for each artefact. The exemplar includes captions for: Early Level Sketch (Zone 1 Layout), Prototype V1 Screenshot (Basic Mechanics), Prototype V2 Screenshot (Expanded Mechanics), Peer Testing Feedback Table (WWW/EBI), Impact vs Effort Matrix, and Before/After – Hint System Implementation.

Optional Stretch Activity – AI Battle Reflection & Documentation (10 minutes): Students document their findings from the AI Battle challenge, comparing the performance of AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) on a game development prompt. Students rate each AI 1–5, compare responses, identify weaknesses both AIs missed, and reflect on how they might use AI tools in their own game development.

Reflection / Plenary – Design Journey Summary: Students reflect on their Term 2 journey and how their portfolio demonstrates progression from idea to refined prototype. Students complete My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment.

From the Lesson 12 exemplar: Portfolio Entry 14A – Evaluation Table: Students complete a full evaluation of their progress across each stage of the design cycle (Empathise, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test & Evaluate), rating each stage 1–5 and identifying what they did well, what to improve, and supporting evidence. Portfolio Entry 14B – SMART Goals for Term 3 (15 minutes): Students select 2–3 areas and write a SMART goal for each — one sentence following the Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound criteria — with steps to achieve it and a deadline. Example goals cover Visual/Atmosphere, Testing/Feedback, and Technical/Mechanics. Portfolio Entry 15 – Term 2 Final Prototype: Students upload final prototype screenshots or a gameplay clip and describe in one sentence what improved most in their final version.

teacher_notes.notes: Use bullet points or short notes.

rows: Which AI tool did you use for the challenge? (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, etc.)
ChatGPT, Claude
What prompt did you give the AI to complete the challenge?
"Give me one suggestion to improve my game or portfolio this week. My game is about collecting light crystals in Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque and players said they can't find the hidden crystals."
How well did the AI perform? Rate 1-5 and explain.
ChatGPT: 4/5 - gave a good idea but was a bit generic. Claude: 4/5 - more specific to Endstar but longer answer than I needed.
What surprised you about the AI's response?
ChatGPT suggested adding a "glowing particle effect" which isn't possible in Endstar. Claude knew about Trigger Volumes and Dialogue Nodes which actually exist in the engine.
Compare: Which suggestion was clearer or more helpful?
Claude's was more helpful because it suggested using Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue, which I can actually build. ChatGPT's particle effect idea sounded cool but isn't realistic for Endstar.
Compare: Which critique spotted real weaknesses?
Claude spotted that my hint might be "too obvious" and suggested making it poetic instead of direct. ChatGPT just said "make sure it fits the theme" which is vague.
Compare: Which AI gave the most useful improvement?
Claude gave more useful improvement because it suggested specific wiring (Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node with OnTriggered → GiveInstruction) that I could copy straight into my game.
Declare your winner: AI System 1 or AI System 2 - and explain why
Winner: Claude - It understood what Endstar can actually do and gave me a suggestion I could implement right away. ChatGPT's ideas were creative but not realistic for my platform
What weakness did both AIs miss?
Neither AI mentioned that I should test the Trigger Volume size - if it's too small players might walk past it, if it's too big the hint triggers too early. I had to figure that out myself.
How might you use AI tools in your game development project?

I'll use AI to help write NPC dialogue that sounds natural, debug wiring problems when props don't connect properly, and get feedback on my portfolio captions. But I'll always check if the suggestions actually work in Endstar before implementing them.
Empathise

Could have asked more classmates what they actually want in a mosque game before starting. Next time I'll do actual interviews.
Early brainstorm notes, player persona sketches
Define
Created clear game concept - collect 5 hidden crystals while exploring mosque zones. Focused on one main mechanic so it wouldn't get too complicated.
My problem statement was a bit vague at first. Took me time to narrow down exactly what makes it educational AND fun.
Design document, problem statement draft
Ideate
Generated lots of ideas for puzzles - lever + counter, pressure plate + timed door, NPC hints. Drew multiple sketches before deciding. Used paper prototyping to test ideas.
Sometimes I got too excited and wanted to add everything. Need to be better at picking only the best 2-3 ideas.
Paper prototypes, brainstorm sketches, idea maps
Prototype
Built 3 versions! V1 was basic, V2 added puzzles, V3 added hint system with Wise Elder NPC after feedback. Each version got better. Used proper Endstar wiring.
V1 took too long because I was learning Endstar. Should have done simpler test first. Some wiring got messy.
Screenshot of V1, V2, V3 builds in Endstar
Test & Evaluate
Got feedback from Khalid, Fatima, and Layla. Actually listened and changed things - added hint NPC because of Khalid's feedback about confusing crystal locations. Used impact vs effort to prioritize.
Could have tested more often instead of waiting until the end. Should test after every small change.
WWW/EBI feedback forms, Impact vs Effort matrix

I will improve the mosque atmosphere by adding 3 new decorative props and adjusting Ambient Settings lighting in each zone by Week 2 of Term 3.

1. Browse Endstar extended library for Islamic-style props 
2. Place decorations in each zone 
3. Use Inspector to adjust Ambient Settings for warmer lighting
Week 2, Term 3
Testing / Feedback
I will test my game with 4 different players and collect WWW/EBI feedback after each major change before the final pitch.

1. Create simple feedback form 
2. Schedule one test per week with classmates 
3. Record feedback and pick one change per session
Week 4, Term 3
Technical / Mechanics
I will add one new puzzle using a Counter Rule and 3 Levers where players must pull all levers to open a locked door in Zone 3 by Week 3 of Term 3.

1. Paper prototype the lever puzzle layout first 
2. Place 3 Levers and wire each OnFlipped→ Counter Rule ChangeCount 
3. Wire Counter OnCountReached → Door Open 4. Test with 2 players to check if it's clear
