metadata.title: Reflection & Next Steps — From Evaluation to Vision
core_topics: Design cycle evaluation (Empathise, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test & Evaluate)
Product Development Cycle – Test & Evaluate phase
Transition to Refine, Market & Pitch phase
AI literacy and critical evaluation of AI outputs
AI tool comparison and critical analysis
ai_focus: Critical evaluation of AI outputs: AI outputs should be constrained, critiqued, and evaluated by humans
AI tool comparison: comparing ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini on game improvement prompts
NotebookLM for generating narrated videos, summaries, cue cards, audio lessons, revision packs, and pitch scripts
AI literacy reflection: declaring AI use, checking AI output, and applying personal judgement
Generative AI producing misleading content including intermediate reasoning steps (OECD K4.3)
AI operating without intent or understanding, requiring human oversight (OECD K1.2)
Societal and ethical risks of AI beyond individual use (OECD K5.1)
Managing AI in the design process: using AI to suggest portfolio evidence mapping without writing the evaluation
Using AI for NPC dialogue writing, debugging wiring problems, and portfolio caption feedback in game development
Recognising AI limitations: suggestions that are creative but not feasible for a specific platform (e.g. Endstar)
learning_objectives: Evaluate your Term 2 progress across the design cycle.
Set clear, achievable SMART goals for Term 3 (Refine & Pitch).
metadata.activity_description:
The lesson opens with a Starter: Before & After activity where students place an early prototype version beside their final Term 2 build, identify what changed, improved, and stayed the same, then turn to a partner and share one key improvement and explain why it mattered.

Students then Explore: Evaluating Our Design Journey by rating each of the five Product Development Cycle stages (Empathise, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test & Evaluate) on a 1–5 scale and justifying their rating with specific examples. An AI prompt is modelled for students: 'I will share my ratings and short notes. Tell me which pieces of my portfolio (screenshots, prototypes, test notes) best match each stage. Do not write the evaluation — only suggest which evidence fits where.'

Portfolio Entry 14A – Evaluation Table (Assessment of Learning): Students complete a full self-evaluation table covering all five design cycle stages, rating each 1–5 and identifying what they did well, what they need to improve, and which existing portfolio artefacts support each score.

Students Analyse: Goal Setting for Term 3, reviewing model examples of vague versus strong SMART goals and four focus areas for Term 3 (visual polish, user testing, presentation skills, technical implementation).

Portfolio Entry 14B – SMART Goals for Term 3 (Assessment of Learning, 15 minutes): Students choose 2–3 areas (Visual/Atmosphere, Testing/Feedback, Technical/Mechanics, or their own) and write one SMART goal sentence per area along with steps to achieve it and a deadline.

Portfolio Entry 15 – Final Prototype (Assessment of Learning): Students upload screenshots or a gameplay clip of their final Term 2 prototype and write one sentence describing what improved most and why it matters.

A Portfolio Check slide guides students through all 15 portfolio entries (Entries 1–15) to ensure their end-of-term portfolio is complete before Term 3.

Stretch Activity – Introduction to NotebookLM: Students open NotebookLM (notebooklm.google.com), create a new notebook, upload 2–3 pieces of work (prototype screenshot, reflection paragraph, persona/empathy map), and use NotebookLM Studio to generate a 45–60 second narrated video of their Term 2 game design journey using the prompt: 'Create a 45–60 second narrated video explaining my Term 2 game design journey: what I built, what changed, and what I will refine in Term 3.' Students then export the video and optionally add the link to their portfolio.

Reflect: Design Journey Summary – Students write 2–3 sentences about their strongest skill this term and what they will improve in Term 3, plus complete an AI Literacy Reflection using sentence starters ('Today AI helped me by…' and 'I still needed to decide…').

Extension/Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson: Students write a 60-word paragraph on how they used AI this term (research, ideas, feedback, or polishing), including one benefit and one limitation, with a supporting screenshot of AI output added to the portfolio.

Extension/Adaptive Task – Endstar Build Time: Students make final updates in Endstar and capture last screenshots for their portfolio.

The lesson closes with three AI Literacy MCQ Learning Check questions (MCQ 12.1–12.3) to be completed in Classkick and a Self-Assessment slide covering All/Most/Some differentiated success criteria.

Optional Stretch Activity from Exemplar Work (Lesson 11–12, 10 minutes) – AI Battle Reflection & Documentation: Students compare two AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT and Claude) on a game improvement prompt, rate each tool's performance, compare outputs, declare a winner, and reflect on what both AIs missed and how they might use AI in future game development.

rows: Which AI tool did you use for the challenge? (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, etc.)
ChatGPT, Claude
What prompt did you give the AI to complete the challenge?
"Give me one suggestion to improve my game or portfolio this week. My game is about collecting light crystals in Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque and players said they can't find the hidden crystals."
How well did the AI perform? Rate 1-5 and explain.
ChatGPT: 4/5 - gave a good idea but was a bit generic. Claude: 4/5 - more specific to Endstar but longer answer than I needed.
What surprised you about the AI's response?
ChatGPT suggested adding a "glowing particle effect" which isn't possible in Endstar. Claude knew about Trigger Volumes and Dialogue Nodes which actually exist in the engine.
Compare: Which suggestion was clearer or more helpful?
Claude's was more helpful because it suggested using Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue, which I can actually build. ChatGPT's particle effect idea sounded cool but isn't realistic for Endstar.
Compare: Which critique spotted real weaknesses?
Claude spotted that my hint might be "too obvious" and suggested making it poetic instead of direct. ChatGPT just said "make sure it fits the theme" which is vague.
Compare: Which AI gave the most useful improvement?
Claude gave more useful improvement because it suggested specific wiring (Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node with OnTriggered → GiveInstruction) that I could copy straight into my game.
Declare your winner: AI System 1 or AI System 2 - and explain why
Winner: Claude - It understood what Endstar can actually do and gave me a suggestion I could implement right away. ChatGPT's ideas were creative but not realistic for my platform
What weakness did both AIs miss?
Neither AI mentioned that I should test the Trigger Volume size - if it's too small players might walk past it, if it's too big the hint triggers too early. I had to figure that out myself.
How might you use AI tools in your game development project?

I'll use AI to help write NPC dialogue that sounds natural, debug wiring problems when props don't connect properly, and get feedback on my portfolio captions. But I'll always check if the suggestions actually work in Endstar before implementing them.
